invariants.rationale: Proves `regions` is a real array (not null, undefined, or a plain object). A 200 that serialises `regions` as `null` or `{}` would silently break all downstream region-selection logic.
A negative or missing `count` indicates a counting regression; pagination consumers rely on this field to know when to stop fetching pages.
A `limit` of 0 would make the page-size denominator unusable and signals a broken pagination default. Status 200 alone cannot reveal this.
A negative `offset` is semantically invalid and indicates the response envelope has corrupted pagination metadata.
Proves `products` is a real array. A regression that returns `products: null` or omits the key entirely would still yield HTTP 200 but silently break every storefront listing.
`estimate_count` is a Medusa v2-only envelope field. Its absence indicates the request was served by a v1 handler or a route mismatch, which a status-200 check cannot detect.
A negative or missing `count` signals a counting regression; storefronts use this for pagination math and result-set size display.
A zero `limit` means no products were requested or the default page-size configuration is broken; undetectable from the status code alone.
A negative `offset` is semantically invalid for a cursor-based browse flow and reveals corrupt pagination metadata despite a 200 status.

656408701148a5844aa1d08771211f1eca356c37.flow_name: Guest Product Browse and Detail View

invariants.rationale: A 200 that returns a null or absent product envelope is a silent failure the status code cannot catch; this guards against serialization regressions that drop the top-level object.
Every persisted product must carry a stable identifier; an undefined id indicates a data-mapping or ORM regression even when HTTP 200 is returned.
A product with a missing or empty title is effectively uncatalogued; this catches silent content-serialization regressions that still yield 200.
The products array must always be present on a successful list response; its absence signals a schema-serialization regression invisible to the status assertion.

Both surrounding detail steps return 200 for a specific product, proving at least one published product exists; an empty list here indicates a visibility, sales-channel, or region filtering regression.
A zero or negative limit breaks pagination semantics for downstream consumers; this invariant catches misconfigured or regressed pagination defaults.
Offset must be non-negative; a negative value signals a corrupted pagination state that a 200 status code cannot surface.
Total catalog count cannot be negative; a negative value indicates a broken aggregation query whose error is masked by the 200 status.
Confirms a real, non-empty credential was issued; HTTP 200 alone cannot prove the token field is present and usable for subsequent admin calls.
count must be a non-negative integer; a missing or negative value signals a broken aggregation query even when the envelope returns 200.
The test fixture must contain at least one order (the one to be fulfilled); an empty array with HTTP 200 means the order was not persisted or the admin token has no visibility into it.
limit must be a positive integer for pagination to be meaningful; zero or missing breaks every downstream page-size calculation.
offset must be zero or positive; a negative value signals a pagination regression that HTTP 200 cannot surface.
The fetched order must carry its own id; absence indicates a serialization failure even if the status code is 200.
Before any fulfillment is created the order must be in the 'not_fulfilled' state; any other value means the test fixture has an invalid pre-condition and the POST step's assertion cannot be trusted.
Verifies financial totals on the fetched order are internally consistent before the fulfillment mutation; a corrupt total that 200s here would silently propagate to the fulfilled order.
The returned payload must identify the order so callers can confirm the response body belongs to the order that was just mutated, not a stub.

After fulfilling all order items, fulfillment_status must transition to 'fulfilled'; receiving 'not_fulfilled' or 'partially_fulfilled' with HTTP 200 is a silent workflow failure—the core behavioral regression this test must catch.

The fulfillments array on the returned order must contain at least one entry; an empty array with HTTP 200 means the fulfillment record was not created or was not serialized into the response, making the status-code assertion meaningless.

db693e9aa4a0260c4cad88457d55fe515b545679.flow_name: Admin Product Catalogue and Order Overview with Product Update

invariants.rationale: A 200 with a missing or empty token still passes the status-code assertion but leaves every downstream admin call unauthenticated; the template confirms the token field is present and non-empty.

The flow immediately fetches a specific product by ID and later updates it, so the catalogue must be non-empty; an empty array on 200 would indicate a data-setup or query regression invisible to the status check.
count must be consistent with a non-empty products array; a count of 0 while items are returned exposes a pagination-metadata regression that callers use for total-page calculations.
An unpaginated first-page request must echo offset 0; any other value means the server applied an unexpected default cursor and callers would skip leading records.
A zero or negative limit would make every page silently appear empty regardless of catalogue size; this guards the default-limit configuration.
A 200 that returns a product shell without an id means the record was not resolved correctly; any subsequent update call would target the wrong or nonexistent resource.
A product without a title is an incomplete record; a null or empty-string title on 200 is a silent serializer regression the status-code assertion cannot detect.
Variants are a mandatory sub-resource of every Medusa product; their absence on a successful GET signals a broken eager-load or serializer regression.
A purchasable product must have at least one variant; zero variants silently breaks downstream order-creation and inventory flows that depend on variant IDs even though the endpoint returns 200.
The orders key must be present in the envelope even when the list is empty; its absence indicates an envelope-shape regression that callers treating the field as an array would crash on.
count must be a non-negative integer; undefined or negative signals a broken aggregation query that the 200 status does not surface.
A default first-page orders request must echo offset 0; a non-zero default means records are skipped without any pagination parameter having been sent.
A positive limit must always be echoed back; 0 would suppress all order records from the response regardless of stored data.
The customers array must exist in the envelope even when empty; a missing key is a silent serializer regression invisible to the status-code assertion.
count must be a non-negative integer; an undefined or negative value reveals a broken aggregation query that 200 alone cannot surface.
A default first-page request must echo offset 0; a non-zero value means customers are silently skipped without any pagination parameter being sent.
The default page limit must be positive; 0 would suppress all customer records from the response despite them existing in the database.
The update endpoint must return the persisted product with its ID; a missing ID means the response body is not a valid product record and the success of the write is unverifiable.

A successful product update must produce a non-null updated_at timestamp; a null or absent value is the strongest indicator that the write was not actually committed to the database even though the server returned 200.

The update response must include the variants sub-resource to confirm the serializer completed a full product hydration; an absent variants key suggests partial or failed serialization of the post-write state.

5604e6c87f21cc8e3d4a723cd73f6acefaf26ab3.flow_name: Registered Customer Browses, Authenticates, Builds Cart, Hits Cart Update Error

invariants.rationale: A HTTP 400 from POST /store/carts/{id} is produced by business-logic validation inside the workflow (e.g. country code not belonging to the supplied region_id). The Medusa error envelope sets type:'invalid_data' for these cases — distinguishing them from auth-gate rejections (type:'unauthorized', 401) and missing-resource errors (type:'not_found', 404). Asserting this value proves the cart was rejected for the correct semantic reason, not merely that some non-2xx was returned.

The Medusa error envelope always populates 'message' with a non-empty human-readable explanation of the specific violation (e.g. 'Country with code X is not within region Y'). A truthy assertion confirms the server identified and articulated a concrete failure cause rather than returning a blank or structurally malformed error body.

959423677e0fa2fb5f4216b2f8bbb329f913f4a8.flow_name: registered customer hits an error path

invariants.rationale: The whole response body must conform to the auth-failure error envelope, proving the 401 originates from a credential rejection (wrong password / unknown identity) rather than a routing misconfiguration or an internal 500 that was accidentally downgraded.

Medusa's emailpass provider always surfaces a non-empty human-readable message on a 401 so that callers can distinguish an invalid-credentials rejection from a silent or malformed error; an absent or empty message would indicate the wrong code path was hit.

A checkout journey requires at least one purchasable product; a 200 with an empty products array would make every downstream item-add step impossible and signals a catalog or sales-channel misconfiguration.
count must be consistent with a non-empty catalog; count=0 while products is non-empty reveals an aggregation or index bug that would corrupt pagination.
The first product listing page must report offset=0; a non-zero offset on an unpaged call indicates the query cursor is not reset correctly.

A 200 must carry a usable JWT token; without asserting the token field, a 200 with a missing or empty token would silently fail every auth-gated downstream call (POST /store/carts*, POST /store/payment-collections*) with a 401.
The success discriminator requires cart.id to be present; a missing id means the cart was not persisted and every subsequent step that references {id} in the URL would 404.
A cart must resolve and record a region_id at creation; absent region_id means regional pricing, tax lines, and currency cannot be computed in any later step.
currency_code is derived from the resolved region at cart creation; an absent value means item prices and order totals would be currency-less, breaking payment provider authorization.
The sales channel must be resolved and stored; without sales_channel_id the confirmVariantInventoryWorkflow in later steps cannot validate availability.
Even an empty, just-created cart must satisfy the totals balance invariant; a discrepancy here would propagate silently through every subsequent recalculation step.
After a successful add-line-item call the cart must contain at least one item; a 200 with an empty items array means createLineItemsStep silently failed to persist the item.
Adding a priced variant must produce a positive item_total after refreshCartItemsWorkflow; a zero value means the variant's calculated_price was not applied or the item was not counted in totals.
refreshCartItemsWorkflow reruns after the add; the balance invariant catches cases where item_total changed but cart.total was not updated consistently.
The cart update must return the same cart object; a missing id means the response body is not a valid cart and the cart reference is lost for subsequent steps.
For a registered-customer flow, the cart update sets the customer's email on the cart; an undefined email means customer association failed and the order cannot be linked to the account.
refreshCartItemsWorkflow reruns on every cart update; a balance violation here would silently corrupt the subtotal/total displayed at checkout and carried into the order.
The checkout flow cannot advance without at least one shipping option; zero results on a 200 would cause the subsequent shipping-methods step to have nothing to select, crashing checkout silently.
This endpoint replaces all shipping methods; a 200 with an empty shipping_methods array means addShippingMethodToCartStep produced no result and validateShippingStep at cart completion will fail.

The selected shipping option's calculated_amount must be folded into cart.shipping_total; a negative or undefined value means the refreshCartItemsWorkflow did not propagate the shipping cost into cart totals.
After adding a shipping method, refreshCartItemsWorkflow updates all totals; the balance invariant catches cases where shipping_total was set but cart.total was not updated to include it.
A created payment collection must carry its id; without it the subsequent POST /store/payment-collections/{id}/payment-sessions step cannot reference the collection and no session can be created.
The payment collection must inherit the cart's currency_code; an absent value means the provider cannot denominate the authorization amount and payment will fail at completion.
At least one payment provider must be enabled; zero providers means no payment session can be created and cart completion will be blocked at validateCartPaymentsStep.

count must reflect a non-empty provider set; count=0 while payment_providers is non-empty reveals an aggregation bug; count>0 with an empty list reveals a retrieval bug — either silently breaks the next step.

After this call, payment_sessions must be non-empty; an empty array on a 200 means the session was not persisted, and validateCartPaymentsStep at cart completion will reject the cart with no clear error.

Cart completion returns HTTP 200 on BOTH success (type='order') and payment-authorization failure (type='cart', error set); this template asserts the order branch was taken — the only invariant that distinguishes a real order from a silent payment failure.

type is the sole discriminator between a placed order and a failed payment on a 200 response; type='cart' means authorization failed and no order exists — a status-code assertion alone cannot detect this.
A newly placed order must be in 'pending' status; any other value (e.g. 'canceled', 'completed') would indicate an unexpected post-placement workflow transition that would break fulfillment flows.
The fetched order must carry its persisted id; a missing id means the response is not a valid order object, regardless of HTTP 200.

Immediately after placement the order must still be 'pending'; a different status would indicate an unexpected automated transition (e.g. auto-capture or auto-cancel) occurred between placement and this read.
The placed order must carry the cart's line items; an empty items array means the cart→order item linkage was lost during the createCartsToOrderStep conversion, making the order unfulfillable.
Order totals must be self-consistent as persisted; a balance violation here means the financial summary used for invoicing and fulfillment is corrupt and was not caught at placement time.
At least one region must exist; an empty array with HTTP 200 is a silent catalog failure that causes cart creation to throw NOT_FOUND, making the rest of the journey unreachable.
count must be non-zero when regions are present; a zero count alongside a non-empty regions array reveals a pagination metadata regression independent of the list contents.

A 200 without a bearer token leaves the customer unable to pass the requireCustomerAuth gate (GATE_MATCHERS: /store/carts*, /store/payment-collections*), silently blocking every subsequent mutation in the journey.
The flow requires at least one purchasable product; HTTP 200 with an empty list is a silent catalog failure that makes variant selection impossible.
The top-level 'product' key must be present; its absence at HTTP 200 is a serialization regression that would leave downstream steps with no product data.
product.id must be a non-empty string so that the correct variant can be associated with this product when building the cart.
At least one variant must be present; a 200 with an empty variants array silently prevents any line-item from being added to the cart.
cart.id (a cart_* string) must be present so subsequent POST /store/carts/{id} and complete calls can reference the correct cart.
The handler resolves a region before creating the cart; a missing region_id with HTTP 200 means the region guard silently passed without binding a region, breaking currency and price resolution.
currency_code is derived from the resolved region; its absence means all line-item prices will be undefined and totals cannot be computed.

A successfully validated sales channel must be bound to the cart; absence means validateSalesChannelStep completed without persisting the channel, which will cause subsequent inventory checks to reference no channel.
Items submitted in the creation payload must appear in cart.items; an empty items array with HTTP 200 means the line-item creation step was silently skipped.
The updated cart must echo its id; absence signals a serialization regression in the update handler response.
For a registered customer (has_account === true), email must be persisted on the cart after update; a missing email means the customer association step failed silently despite HTTP 200.
Line items must survive the cart update; an empty items array here will cause validateCartItemsStep to block completion even though all prior steps returned 200.

After refreshCartItemsWorkflow re-derives prices, promotions, and tax lines, the cart's subtotal/tax_total/discount_total/total must be internally consistent; mismatched totals at HTTP 200 are a silent pricing regression.
At least one shipping option must be available for the cart; an empty list with HTTP 200 means validateShippingStep will block cart completion with no actionable error visible here.
The created payment collection must have an id so the subsequent POST /store/payment-collections/{id}/payment-sessions call can reference the correct collection.
At least one payment provider must be registered; an empty list with HTTP 200 means no payment session can be created and checkout is silently blocked.
At least one payment session must exist after this call; an empty sessions array means validateCartPaymentsStep will find nothing to authorize and will block cart completion.

HTTP 200 alone cannot distinguish a placed order from a payment-authorization failure; the body must confirm type === 'order' with a populated order object — type === 'cart' (with an error field) is the same-status failure shape.
type === 'cart' is the HTTP-200 failure envelope (payment-auth failed, cart not converted); asserting 'order' is the only body-level proof that the cart was actually converted to an order.
order.id must be a non-empty string so the subsequent GET /store/orders/{id} retrieval targets the correct, newly placed order.

A newly placed order must carry status 'pending'; any other value ('completed', 'canceled', etc.) indicates the order lifecycle was incorrectly advanced or the idempotent re-entry path returned a stale order.
The retrieved order must have a populated id; absence with HTTP 200 signals a serialization regression or that the wrong resource was resolved by the id.
A freshly placed order must remain 'pending' at retrieval; a different status indicates the order lifecycle was incorrectly advanced between placement and this read.
The placed order must contain at least one line item; an empty items array with HTTP 200 means the cart's products were not transferred to the order during completion.

The finalized order's financial fields must be internally consistent; mismatched subtotal/tax_total/discount_total/total values at HTTP 200 indicate a pricing or serialization regression in order creation.

A successful customer auth must return a usable token; a 200 without a valid token leaves every gated cart/checkout route (GATE_MATCHERS: /store/carts*, /store/payment-collections*) inaccessible, silently breaking all downstream steps.
The product object must carry its id; downstream steps reference the product's variant ids when constructing line items.
At least one variant must exist so a line item can be added in the next step; zero variants means checkout cannot proceed regardless of status code.
The created cart must be assigned an id; every subsequent mutating step embeds this id in the URL path — an absent id means the cart was never persisted.
The endpoint behavior notes that the workflow throws NOT_FOUND when no region resolves; a defined region_id on the response proves the region guard was satisfied and prices can be computed.
Currency code must be set (from request or region default) for all price computations in subsequent steps; an absent currency_code means line-item prices cannot be resolved.
A resolved sales channel must be stored on the cart; it is required by inventory and availability checks when adding line items.
Even a newly created cart must have internally consistent totals; an imbalance here indicates the tax or discount pipeline is broken before any items are added.

After adding a line item, cart.items must be non-empty; a 200 with zero items means the add silently failed — the status code alone cannot detect this because the body is always a cart object regardless.

A non-zero item_total proves that the added variant has a resolved unit_price and that refreshCartItemsWorkflow recalculated totals; zero here means price resolution silently failed even though an item entry may appear.
After adding items and running refreshCartItemsWorkflow, all cart totals must be internally consistent; an imbalance reveals a regression in the post-add recalculation pipeline.
The updated cart must echo back its id, confirming the correct cart was mutated and returned.
After updating the cart (email, address, region) and running refreshCartItemsWorkflow, totals must remain balanced including any repriced items; an imbalance here is a silent pricing regression.

At least one shipping option must be available for the cart's region/sales-channel context; zero options means the next step has no valid option to select and the validateShippingStep will block order placement.

The workflow replaces all prior shipping methods then adds the submitted one(s); zero methods on the returned cart means neither the replacement nor the add was persisted — validateShippingStep at completion will fail.
After adding a method with a calculated_price, shipping_total must be a non-negative number; this proves the method's calculated_amount was folded into totals by refreshCartItemsWorkflow.
After replacing shipping methods and refreshing the cart, totals including the new shipping amount must be balanced; an imbalance reveals the refresh did not run or the amount was not folded in.
The payment collection must be created with an id; subsequent steps pass this id in the URL to create payment sessions and the cart-complete step links order to it.
At least one payment provider must be available; without one, the next step cannot select a provider for the payment session and validateCartPaymentsStep will block order placement.
The count field must reflect a non-zero available provider count; a count of 0 while payment_providers is non-empty (or vice versa) reveals a pagination or aggregation regression.

After creating a payment session, the collection must contain at least one session; the cart-complete step's validateCartPaymentsStep requires an authorizable session and will surface as a 4xx if none exists.
The payment collection must retain its id after sessions are added to it; a missing id means the returned object is not the expected collection.

This endpoint returns HTTP 200 for BOTH success (type='order') and payment-authorization failure (type='cart'); asserting type='order' is the only way to prove the cart was actually converted to an order — the status code cannot distinguish these outcomes.
The template holistically asserts the success envelope — type='order' plus a valid order object — ruling out the payment-failure envelope (type='cart', no order) that also returns HTTP 200.
A newly placed order must have status 'pending'; any other status (e.g. 'completed' or 'canceled') signals an unexpected state transition during placement.
A successfully placed order must carry an id; this id is used in the subsequent GET step to retrieve and verify the order.
The retrieved order must carry its id, confirming it was persisted and is retrievable after placement.
A newly placed order must still be in 'pending' status when subsequently retrieved; any other status (e.g. 'canceled') signals an unintended side-effect during or after order creation.
The placed order must contain at least the line items transferred from the cart; zero items means items were silently dropped during the cart-to-order conversion.
The order's totals must be internally consistent; an imbalance here reveals a silent pricing or discount regression that occurred during order creation from the cart.
The checkout flow requires at least one active region; a zero-length array means POST /store/carts will throw 'No regions found' and every subsequent step is unreachable.
The server-reported count must agree that regions exist; a count/regions.length discrepancy exposes a pagination or serialization bug invisible to the status code.

HTTP 200 does not prove a usable credential was issued; the token must be present and non-empty because all subsequent POST/PATCH operations on /store/carts* and /store/payment-collections* are gated by requireCustomerAuth and will return 401 without it.
A non-empty catalog is a prerequisite for selecting a valid variant_id; an empty list means the line-item step will fail with a not_found or invalid_data error for any variant chosen.
cart.id must be present and stable because every subsequent step addresses the cart by this id; its absence proves the cart was not persisted despite the 200.
The resolved region must be attached to the cart; a missing region_id means currency and tax computation are undefined and line-item pricing will silently fail downstream.
Currency must be set from the resolved region; without it variant prices cannot be computed in a consistent denomination in the line-item step.
The sales channel must be attached; inventory and price validation in POST /store/carts/{id}/line-items keys off this field, so its absence silently breaks availability checks.
The response is always a cart object regardless of whether the item was actually added; asserting cart.items.length > 0 is the only body-level proof that the line-item mutation took effect.

A successfully added item must produce a non-zero item_total; a zero value means the variant's price was not resolved—the item exists in the cart but carries no monetary value, causing the order total to be wrong at completion.

For a registered customer, the email must be echoed back on the updated cart; its absence means the customer context was not linked, which would produce an anonymous order or break order-confirmation emails.

The shipping address must be persisted in this update step; an absent address predicts a downstream failure in validateShippingStep during cart completion, and a 200 here cannot reveal that the field was silently dropped.

At least one shipping option must exist for the cart's region/sales channel; validateShippingStep during POST /store/carts/{id}/complete will block order placement if no valid shipping method is available, so an empty list here is a guaranteed future failure.
A payment collection must be created with a stable id; without it the payment-session step has no collection to attach to and validateCartPaymentsStep during completion will block order placement.
The payment collection must reference the cart it was created for; an absent cart_id means the collection is orphaned and will not be recognized by the completion workflow's payment validation.
At least one payment provider must be configured; without a provider the next step cannot create a payment session and cart completion is impossible regardless of HTTP status.

A payment session must exist on the collection; validateCartPaymentsStep during cart completion will reject the cart with a 4xx if no active session is present, and this 200 cannot prove the session was actually created without inspecting the array.

Cart completion returns HTTP 200 for both successful placement ({ type: 'order', order }) and payment-authorization failure ({ type: 'cart', error }); asserting type === 'order' is the only way to distinguish the two outcomes—the status code alone cannot.
A newly placed order must be in 'pending' status; any other value (e.g. 'completed' or 'canceled') indicates an unintended post-placement state transition in the order lifecycle event chain.
The order must be retrievable by the id returned from cart completion; an absent id means the order was not durably persisted or the id in the completion response is stale or mismatched.

The persisted order status must match what cart completion reported; a discrepancy (e.g. 'canceled') proves the order was mutated between placement and retrieval by a rogue side-effect or event handler.
